Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社研究会|zh|式内社研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) (also known as Amenohaikazuchi-no-Mikoto) * ''{{ill|Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|en|Tanabatabime-no-Mikoto|qq=draft}}'' (also known as Amanoorihime) {{citation needed|Shidori Shrine (Tottori)|qq=draft}} }} {{Multiple issues |accuracy=2016-10 |notability=2016-10 }} {{Infobox Shinto shrine |name = '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) |image = [[File:Shitori-jinjya-totori-honden.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|26|25.6|N|134|12|1.9|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}{{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鸟取市|qq=draft}} Oaza Shitori Ie-no-Ue 548-1 |ISO = JP-31 |latitude = 35/26/25.6/N |longitude = 134/12/1.9/E |label = left |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}})
{{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}} |goshintai = |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|zh|式内社|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}} |foundation = Unknown |main_hall = Ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} |other_names = |pilgrimage = |annual_festival = October 9 |events = |map = Japan Tottori }} '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto-Schrein|ja|神社|zh|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori area. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|zh|式内社|qq=draft}}, and under the modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines ({{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|de|Modernes System der Shinto-Schreineinstufung|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}) it was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}}. The shrine stands on the western slope of the mountain in the Shitori settlement, located in the southwestern part of Tottori City. == history == The exact date of the shrine’s founding (勧請) is unknown, but it is said to have originated when members of the {{ill|Shitori/Shizuri clan|de|Shitori/ Shizuri-Clan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} enshrined their ancestral deity. It is counted among the seven shrines of {{ill|Takakusa District|de|Takakusa Distrikt|ja|高草郡|zh|高草郡|qq=draft}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|zh|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}. In the medieval period and beyond, because the enshrined deity {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}}) was said to have seven names, it became known as the “Seven-Body Great Deity” (七躰大明神, Nanatai Daimyōjin) and was revered as the local tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|de|Ubusunagami|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|zh|産土神|qq=draft}}). During the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Takeda Takanobu|de|Takeda Takanobu|ja|武田高信|zh|武田高信|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Tamatsu Castle|de|Tamatsu Burg|ja|玉津城|zh|玉津城|qq=draft}}, built a “Three-foot” sub-shrine; in the Keichō era (1598–1615), {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Kano Castle (Tottori)|de|Burg Kano|ja|鹿野城|1=Shikano Castle|zh|鹿野城|qq=draft}}, offered a treasured sword; and in Kan’ei 9 (1632), domain lord {{ill|Ikeda Mitsunaka|de|Ikeda Mitsunaka|ja|池田光仲|zh|池田光仲|qq=draft}} donated 1 koku, 3 to, 1 shō, and 7 gō of shrine land. It thus gained reverence as the guardian deity of the southwestern direction (坤) of {{ill|Tottori Castle|de|Burg Tottori|ja|鳥取城|zh|鳥取城|qq=draft}}. During the Edo period, not only the feudal lords but also the common people held it in high regard. For instance, in the relocation of the shrine in Meiwa 1 (1764), the headman (大庄屋) contributed rice bales on behalf of the entire district, and in Bunka 7 (1810), the district commissioner (郡奉行) donated 100 momme of silver. Many local worshipers gathered at the Setsubun festival, and this popular devotion continued throughout the Inaba region until the early Shōwa period. In 1872 (Meiji 1), the shrine name was changed to “Shitori Jinja”; in 1874 (Meiji 4), it was Ranked as a village shrine; in 1915 (Taishō 4), it was designated a shrine for which the state offered shared expenses for rituals and offerings; in 1919 (Taishō 8), the main hall, hall of offerings, worship hall, and shrine office were renovated; and on October 1, 1943 (Shōwa 18), it was elevated to Gōsha status.Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) The word “Shitori” (also read “Shizuri”) refers to silk textiles. According to tradition, the {{ill|Shitori clan|de|Shitori-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}}, a community engaged in weaving, lived in this area and enshrined their ancestor deity, “Shitori no Kami / Takehazuchi no Mikoto.” The four deities enshrined are {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|經津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}}. In the myth “{{ill|The Hare of Inaba|de|Der Hase von Inaba|ja|因幡の白兎|zh|因幡の白兎|qq=draft}},” it is said that after helping the white hare, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto wrote a love letter here to the Princess Yagami. (It is believed that this actually refers to composing poetry at the time.) It is said that the love letter from Ōkuninushi no Mikoto was associated with the character for “文” (letter or text) and also with rolls of silk cloth.Shrine precinct information sign === Historical Origin === According to the “Continued Chronicles of Japan” ({{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}}), under the entry for Wadō 5 (712) in the seventh month of autumn, it is noted that brocade was woven in Inaba Province. From this, we can infer that even before this year, the descendants of the shrine’s deity {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, who were part of the {{ill|Shitori (Weaving) clan|de|Shitori (Weaving)-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} originally based in the Katsuragi area of Yamato Province and engaged in weaving, had traveled here. They established Shitori Village while continuing their ancestral weaving industry, and then founded this shrine to enshrine their ancestral deity, Takehazuchi no Mikoto.Shrine-owned “YuiShoGaki” (由緒書) === On the Shitori Organization in Inaba and Hōki Provinces === In ancient times, industrial production in {{ill|Inaba Province|de|Provinz Inaba|ja|因幡|zh|因幡|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōki Province|de|Provinz Hōki|ja|伯耆|zh|伯耆|qq=draft}} seems to have been relatively advanced, suggesting these areas were culturally developed. When examining sericulture and the production of silk among the farming population in Inaba and Hōki, it is important to note that these fabrics were produced as tribute ({{ill|Chō (tribute)|de|Chō (Abgabe)|ja|調|zh|調|qq=draft}}), not for daily use by ordinary farmers. Concerning the production of high-grade silk textiles, the entry of Wadō 5 (712) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}} for the seventh month, day of jinwu (壬午), lists 21 provinces, including Ise Province. Both Inaba and Hōki are among them. This confirms that the production of brocade and figured silk (“綾”) was flourishing in Inaba and Hōki from ancient times. “錦部” (Nishikibe) communities, deeply involved in producing brocade and figured silk, can be inferred from the existence of a person named “Nishikibe Shinafu-me” (錦部志奈布女) in Takaniwa-shō in Inaba Province (cited in “Heian Ibun”). Furthermore, the cloth known as “倭文布” (Shizuri or Shitori cloth), said to be a striped textile, also proves the existence of Shitori (倭文) organizations in ancient times. From nationwide distribution maps of Shitori communities created by researchers such as Kamijō Tanenosuke and Inoue Tatsuo, it is evident that they concentrated in the San’in and Tōkai regions. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|de|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|zh|延喜式|qq=draft}}, there are 14 “Shitori Shrines” (倭文神社) recorded nationwide: 6 in the San’in region, 5 in Tōkaidō, 2 in Tōsandō, and 2 in the Kinai. Another 8 shrines related to Shitori but not recorded in the Engishiki exist, with 7 of them in Tōkaidō. In Inaba, both the “Engishiki” Jinmyōchō and the “{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|de|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名抄|zh|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}” note a “Shitori Shrine” (倭文神社) in Takakusa District. The “Heian Ibun” mentions a person named “Shitori Mahiro” (倭文真弘) from Takaniwa-shō. In both Inaba and Hōki, the sites of “Shitori Shrines” often occupy slightly elevated land near coasts, rivers, or lakes—this is true nationwide. Consequently, “Shitori communities” may have had close ties with “Ama (sea-people) communities,” and possibly with the {{ill|Hata clan|de|Hata-Klan|ja|秦氏|zh|秦氏|qq=draft}} in terms of weaving technology. Thus, Shitori communities in Takakusa District’s Takaniwa-shō and in Kawamura District most likely produced high-grade silk. While these high-end products were initially luxuries for the imperial court nobility, over time the technology spread to ordinary people.Kamijō Tanenosuke: “A Preliminary Study on Shitori Communities,” in the journal Nihon Rekishi (日本歴史)Inoue Tatsuo: “A Study of the Shōzei-chō (正税帳)” == Shaden and grounds == [[File:Sukunemizu-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|Shitori no Sukune Mizu]] The main hall is an ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} style with copper roof, rebuilt in Taishō 8 (1919). In the same year, a worship hall (two bays square) was also reconstructed, and the precincts were substantially expanded. The offering hall (幣殿), measuring two bays by one bay, was renovated in Shōwa 12 (1937), and a shrine kitchen (神饌殿) was built in that same year. In Shōwa 17 (1942), a purification font (手水舎) was constructed. There is also a lodging hall (参籠所) and a memorial shrine (祖霊舎) dedicated to the ancestors of parishioners. The precinct covers 747 tsubo (about 2,468 m²); in Shōwa 9 (1934), it measured 469 tsubo (about 1,550 m²). Within the grounds is “Shitori no Sukune Mizu,” said to be a miraculous water especially helpful for women’s health, though now its well is no longer in use. Additionally, near the large De River in front of the shrine there was once a sacred rice field (神饌田) used again as a ritual paddy after some time in private ownership. The first torii, donated by Okamura Ichizaemon in Meiji 2 (1869), is made of stone and stands about 3 meters tall and 2.4 meters wide; until recently it bore a hanging plaque (扁額) reading “倭文神社.” Records show that major reconstructions were carried out from Shōtoku 5 (1715) to Keiō 3 (1867) on 18 occasions, and the roof of the shrine buildings was replaced 13 times.Documents kept by the shrine priest’s family, “Records of Domain Shrine Holdings” (藩主社領帳) === Shrines and sub-shrines within the precincts === * Kaze-no-miya Sha (風宮社) – enshrines the guardian deities of protection from storm and flood. * Soreisha (祖霊舎) – enshrines the ancestors of the parishioners. == Treasures and writings == [[File:Houtou-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|The treasured sword offered by Lord {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}} of Kano Castle]] * One mirror (diameter ~12 cm): donated by Nishio Kihei, forged by Fujiwara Mitsunaga * One sword (total length about 48.5 cm): donated by Kamei Musashino-kami Norisuke ({{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}); believed to be from the Kamakura period * One hanging plaque (width about 90 cm) with raised lettering “倭文神社,” donated by Okamura Juzaemon * Writings: “Thoughts on Shitori no Kami” by Motoori Uchitō * Writings: “On the Deities of Shitori Shrine” by Udagawa Masayoshi == Local information == ; location * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori 548-1 ; Access * About 2 minutes by car from Tottori Minami IC on the {{ill|Tottori Expressway|de|Tottori-Autobahn|ja|鳥取自動車道|zh|鳥取自動車道|qq=draft}} ; neighborhood * {{ill|Kawahara Castle|de|Burg Kawahara|ja|河原城|zh|河原城|qq=draft}} == footnote Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社研究会|zh|式内社研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) {{Multiple issues |accuracy=2016-10 |notability=2016-10 }} {{Infobox Shinto shrine |name = '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) |image = [[File:Shitori-jinjya-totori-honden.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|26|25.6|N|134|12|1.9|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}{{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鸟取市|qq=draft}} Oaza Shitori Ie-no-Ue 548-1 |ISO = JP-31 |latitude = 35/26/25.6/N |longitude = 134/12/1.9/E |label = left |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}})
{{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}} |goshintai = |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}} |foundation = Unknown |main_hall = Ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} |other_names = |pilgrimage = |annual_festival = October 9 |events = |map = Japan Tottori }} '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto-Schrein|ja|神社|zh|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori area. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and under the modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines ({{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|de|Modernes System der Shinto-Schreineinstufung|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}) it was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}}. The shrine stands on the western slope of the mountain in the Shitori settlement, located in the southwestern part of Tottori City. == history == The exact date of the shrine’s founding (勧請) is unknown, but it is said to have originated when members of the {{ill|Shitori/Shizuri clan|de|Shitori/ Shizuri-Clan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} enshrined their ancestral deity. It is counted among the seven shrines of {{ill|Takakusa District|de|Takakusa Distrikt|ja|高草郡|zh|高草郡|qq=draft}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|zh|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}. In the medieval period and beyond, because the enshrined deity {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}}) was said to have seven names, it became known as the “Seven-Body Great Deity” (七躰大明神, Nanatai Daimyōjin) and was revered as the local tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|de|Ubusunagami|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|zh|産土神|qq=draft}}). During the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Takeda Takanobu|de|Takeda Takanobu|ja|武田高信|zh|武田高信|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Tamatsu Castle|de|Tamatsu Burg|ja|玉津城|zh|玉津城|qq=draft}}, built a “Three-foot” sub-shrine; in the Keichō era (1598–1615), {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Kano Castle (Tottori)|de|Burg Kano|ja|鹿野城|1=Shikano Castle|zh|鹿野城|qq=draft}}, offered a treasured sword; and in Kan’ei 9 (1632), domain lord {{ill|Ikeda Mitsunaka|de|Ikeda Mitsunaka|ja|池田光仲|zh|池田光仲|qq=draft}} donated 1 koku, 3 to, 1 shō, and 7 gō of shrine land. It thus gained reverence as the guardian deity of the southwestern direction (坤) of {{ill|Tottori Castle|de|Burg Tottori|ja|鳥取城|zh|鳥取城|qq=draft}}. During the Edo period, not only the feudal lords but also the common people held it in high regard. For instance, in the relocation of the shrine in Meiwa 1 (1764), the headman (大庄屋) contributed rice bales on behalf of the entire district, and in Bunka 7 (1810), the district commissioner (郡奉行) donated 100 momme of silver. Many local worshipers gathered at the Setsubun festival, and this popular devotion continued throughout the Inaba region until the early Shōwa period. In 1872 (Meiji 1), the shrine name was changed to “Shitori Jinja”; in 1874 (Meiji 4), it was Ranked as a village shrine; in 1915 (Taishō 4), it was designated a shrine for which the state offered shared expenses for rituals and offerings; in 1919 (Taishō 8), the main hall, hall of offerings, worship hall, and shrine office were renovated; and on October 1, 1943 (Shōwa 18), it was elevated to Gōsha status.Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) The word “Shitori” (also read “Shizuri”) refers to silk textiles. According to tradition, the {{ill|Shitori clan|de|Shitori-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}}, a community engaged in weaving, lived in this area and enshrined their ancestor deity, “Shitori no Kami / Takehazuchi no Mikoto.” The four deities enshrined are {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|經津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}}. In the myth “{{ill|The Hare of Inaba|de|Der Hase von Inaba|ja|因幡の白兎|zh|因幡の白兎|qq=draft}},” it is said that after helping the white hare, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto wrote a love letter here to the Princess Yagami. (It is believed that this actually refers to composing poetry at the time.) It is said that the love letter from Ōkuninushi no Mikoto was associated with the character for “文” (letter or text) and also with rolls of silk cloth.Shrine precinct information sign === Historical Origin === According to the “Continued Chronicles of Japan” ({{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}}), under the entry for Wadō 5 (712) in the seventh month of autumn, it is noted that brocade was woven in Inaba Province. From this, we can infer that even before this year, the descendants of the shrine’s deity {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, who were part of the {{ill|Shitori (Weaving) clan|de|Shitori (Weaving)-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} originally based in the Katsuragi area of Yamato Province and engaged in weaving, had traveled here. They established Shitori Village while continuing their ancestral weaving industry, and then founded this shrine to enshrine their ancestral deity, Takehazuchi no Mikoto.Shrine-owned “YuiShoGaki” (由緒書) === On the Shitori Organization in Inaba and Hōki Provinces === In ancient times, industrial production in {{ill|Inaba Province|de|Provinz Inaba|ja|因幡|zh|因幡|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōki Province|de|Provinz Hōki|ja|伯耆|zh|伯耆|qq=draft}} seems to have been relatively advanced, suggesting these areas were culturally developed. When examining sericulture and the production of silk among the farming population in Inaba and Hōki, it is important to note that these fabrics were produced as tribute ({{ill|Chō (tribute)|de|Chō (Abgabe)|ja|調|zh|調|qq=draft}}), not for daily use by ordinary farmers. Concerning the production of high-grade silk textiles, the entry of Wadō 5 (712) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}} for the seventh month, day of jinwu (壬午), lists 21 provinces, including Ise Province. Both Inaba and Hōki are among them. This confirms that the production of brocade and figured silk (“綾”) was flourishing in Inaba and Hōki from ancient times. “錦部” (Nishikibe) communities, deeply involved in producing brocade and figured silk, can be inferred from the existence of a person named “Nishikibe Shinafu-me” (錦部志奈布女) in Takaniwa-shō in Inaba Province (cited in “Heian Ibun”). Furthermore, the cloth known as “倭文布” (Shizuri or Shitori cloth), said to be a striped textile, also proves the existence of Shitori (倭文) organizations in ancient times. From nationwide distribution maps of Shitori communities created by researchers such as Kamijō Tanenosuke and Inoue Tatsuo, it is evident that they concentrated in the San’in and Tōkai regions. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|de|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|zh|延喜式|qq=draft}}, there are 14 “Shitori Shrines” (倭文神社) recorded nationwide: 6 in the San’in region, 5 in Tōkaidō, 2 in Tōsandō, and 2 in the Kinai. Another 8 shrines related to Shitori but not recorded in the Engishiki exist, with 7 of them in Tōkaidō. In Inaba, both the “Engishiki” Jinmyōchō and the “{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|de|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名抄|zh|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}” note a “Shitori Shrine” (倭文神社) in Takakusa District. The “Heian Ibun” mentions a person named “Shitori Mahiro” (倭文真弘) from Takaniwa-shō. In both Inaba and Hōki, the sites of “Shitori Shrines” often occupy slightly elevated land near coasts, rivers, or lakes—this is true nationwide. Consequently, “Shitori communities” may have had close ties with “Ama (sea-people) communities,” and possibly with the {{ill|Hata clan|de|Hata-Klan|ja|秦氏|zh|秦氏|qq=draft}} in terms of weaving technology. Thus, Shitori communities in Takakusa District’s Takaniwa-shō and in Kawamura District most likely produced high-grade silk. While these high-end products were initially luxuries for the imperial court nobility, over time the technology spread to ordinary people.Kamijō Tanenosuke: “A Preliminary Study on Shitori Communities,” in the journal Nihon Rekishi (日本歴史)Inoue Tatsuo: “A Study of the Shōzei-chō (正税帳)” == Shaden and grounds == [[File:Sukunemizu-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|Shitori no Sukune Mizu]] The main hall is an ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} style with copper roof, rebuilt in Taishō 8 (1919). In the same year, a worship hall (two bays square) was also reconstructed, and the precincts were substantially expanded. The offering hall (幣殿), measuring two bays by one bay, was renovated in Shōwa 12 (1937), and a shrine kitchen (神饌殿) was built in that same year. In Shōwa 17 (1942), a purification font (手水舎) was constructed. There is also a lodging hall (参籠所) and a memorial shrine (祖霊舎) dedicated to the ancestors of parishioners. The precinct covers 747 tsubo (about 2,468 m²); in Shōwa 9 (1934), it measured 469 tsubo (about 1,550 m²). Within the grounds is “Shitori no Sukune Mizu,” said to be a miraculous water especially helpful for women’s health, though now its well is no longer in use. Additionally, near the large De River in front of the shrine there was once a sacred rice field (神饌田) used again as a ritual paddy after some time in private ownership. The first torii, donated by Okamura Ichizaemon in Meiji 2 (1869), is made of stone and stands about 3 meters tall and 2.4 meters wide; until recently it bore a hanging plaque (扁額) reading “倭文神社.” Records show that major reconstructions were carried out from Shōtoku 5 (1715) to Keiō 3 (1867) on 18 occasions, and the roof of the shrine buildings was replaced 13 times.Documents kept by the shrine priest’s family, “Records of Domain Shrine Holdings” (藩主社領帳) === Shrines and sub-shrines within the precincts === * Kaze-no-miya Sha (風宮社) – enshrines the guardian deities of protection from storm and flood. * Soreisha (祖霊舎) – enshrines the ancestors of the parishioners. == Treasures and writings == [[File:Houtou-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|The treasured sword offered by Lord {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}} of Kano Castle]] * One mirror (diameter ~12 cm): donated by Nishio Kihei, forged by Fujiwara Mitsunaga * One sword (total length about 48.5 cm): donated by Kamei Musashino-kami Norisuke ({{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}); believed to be from the Kamakura period * One hanging plaque (width about 90 cm) with raised lettering “倭文神社,” donated by Okamura Juzaemon * Writings: “Thoughts on Shitori no Kami” by Motoori Uchitō * Writings: “On the Deities of Shitori Shrine” by Udagawa Masayoshi == Local information == ; location * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori 548-1 ; Access * About 2 minutes by car from Tottori Minami IC on the {{ill|Tottori Expressway|de|Tottori-Autobahn|ja|鳥取自動車道|zh|鳥取自動車道|qq=draft}} ; neighborhood * {{ill|Kawahara Castle|de|Burg Kawahara|ja|河原城|zh|河原城|qq=draft}} == footnote Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) {{citation needed|Shidori Shrine (Tottori)|qq=draft}} {{citation {{Multiple issues |accuracy=2016-10 |notability=2016-10 }} {{Infobox Shinto shrine |name = '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) |image = [[File:Shitori-jinjya-totori-honden.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|26|25.6|N|134|12|1.9|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}{{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鸟取市|qq=draft}} Oaza Shitori Ie-no-Ue 548-1 |ISO = JP-31 |latitude = 35/26/25.6/N |longitude = 134/12/1.9/E |label = left |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}})
{{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}} |goshintai = |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}} |foundation = Unknown |main_hall = Ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} |other_names = |pilgrimage = |annual_festival = October 9 |events = |map = Japan Tottori }} '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto-Schrein|ja|神社|zh|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori area. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and under the modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines ({{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|de|Modernes System der Shinto-Schreineinstufung|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}) it was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}}. The shrine stands on the western slope of the mountain in the Shitori settlement, located in the southwestern part of Tottori City. == history == The exact date of the shrine’s founding (勧請) is unknown, but it is said to have originated when members of the {{ill|Shitori/Shizuri clan|de|Shitori/ Shizuri-Clan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} enshrined their ancestral deity. It is counted among the seven shrines of {{ill|Takakusa District|de|Takakusa Distrikt|ja|高草郡|zh|高草郡|qq=draft}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|zh|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}. In the medieval period and beyond, because the enshrined deity {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}}) was said to have seven names, it became known as the “Seven-Body Great Deity” (七躰大明神, Nanatai Daimyōjin) and was revered as the local tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|de|Ubusunagami|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|zh|産土神|qq=draft}}). During the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Takeda Takanobu|de|Takeda Takanobu|ja|武田高信|zh|武田高信|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Tamatsu Castle|de|Tamatsu Burg|ja|玉津城|zh|玉津城|qq=draft}}, built a “Three-foot” sub-shrine; in the Keichō era (1598–1615), {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Kano Castle (Tottori)|de|Burg Kano|ja|鹿野城|1=Shikano Castle|zh|鹿野城|qq=draft}}, offered a treasured sword; and in Kan’ei 9 (1632), domain lord {{ill|Ikeda Mitsunaka|de|Ikeda Mitsunaka|ja|池田光仲|zh|池田光仲|qq=draft}} donated 1 koku, 3 to, 1 shō, and 7 gō of shrine land. It thus gained reverence as the guardian deity of the southwestern direction (坤) of {{ill|Tottori Castle|de|Burg Tottori|ja|鳥取城|zh|鳥取城|qq=draft}}. During the Edo period, not only the feudal lords but also the common people held it in high regard. For instance, in the relocation of the shrine in Meiwa 1 (1764), the headman (大庄屋) contributed rice bales on behalf of the entire district, and in Bunka 7 (1810), the district commissioner (郡奉行) donated 100 momme of silver. Many local worshipers gathered at the Setsubun festival, and this popular devotion continued throughout the Inaba region until the early Shōwa period. In 1872 (Meiji 1), the shrine name was changed to “Shitori Jinja”; in 1874 (Meiji 4), it was Ranked as a village shrine; in 1915 (Taishō 4), it was designated a shrine for which the state offered shared expenses for rituals and offerings; in 1919 (Taishō 8), the main hall, hall of offerings, worship hall, and shrine office were renovated; and on October 1, 1943 (Shōwa 18), it was elevated to Gōsha status.Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) The word “Shitori” (also read “Shizuri”) refers to silk textiles. According to tradition, the {{ill|Shitori clan|de|Shitori-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}}, a community engaged in weaving, lived in this area and enshrined their ancestor deity, “Shitori no Kami / Takehazuchi no Mikoto.” The four deities enshrined are {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|經津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}}. In the myth “{{ill|The Hare of Inaba|de|Der Hase von Inaba|ja|因幡の白兎|zh|因幡の白兎|qq=draft}},” it is said that after helping the white hare, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto wrote a love letter here to the Princess Yagami. (It is believed that this actually refers to composing poetry at the time.) It is said that the love letter from Ōkuninushi no Mikoto was associated with the character for “文” (letter or text) and also with rolls of silk cloth.Shrine precinct information sign === Historical Origin === According to the “Continued Chronicles of Japan” ({{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}}), under the entry for Wadō 5 (712) in the seventh month of autumn, it is noted that brocade was woven in Inaba Province. From this, we can infer that even before this year, the descendants of the shrine’s deity {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, who were part of the {{ill|Shitori (Weaving) clan|de|Shitori (Weaving)-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} originally based in the Katsuragi area of Yamato Province and engaged in weaving, had traveled here. They established Shitori Village while continuing their ancestral weaving industry, and then founded this shrine to enshrine their ancestral deity, Takehazuchi no Mikoto.Shrine-owned “YuiShoGaki” (由緒書) === On the Shitori Organization in Inaba and Hōki Provinces === In ancient times, industrial production in {{ill|Inaba Province|de|Provinz Inaba|ja|因幡|zh|因幡|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōki Province|de|Provinz Hōki|ja|伯耆|zh|伯耆|qq=draft}} seems to have been relatively advanced, suggesting these areas were culturally developed. When examining sericulture and the production of silk among the farming population in Inaba and Hōki, it is important to note that these fabrics were produced as tribute ({{ill|Chō (tribute)|de|Chō (Abgabe)|ja|調|zh|調|qq=draft}}), not for daily use by ordinary farmers. Concerning the production of high-grade silk textiles, the entry of Wadō 5 (712) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}} for the seventh month, day of jinwu (壬午), lists 21 provinces, including Ise Province. Both Inaba and Hōki are among them. This confirms that the production of brocade and figured silk (“綾”) was flourishing in Inaba and Hōki from ancient times. “錦部” (Nishikibe) communities, deeply involved in producing brocade and figured silk, can be inferred from the existence of a person named “Nishikibe Shinafu-me” (錦部志奈布女) in Takaniwa-shō in Inaba Province (cited in “Heian Ibun”). Furthermore, the cloth known as “倭文布” (Shizuri or Shitori cloth), said to be a striped textile, also proves the existence of Shitori (倭文) organizations in ancient times. From nationwide distribution maps of Shitori communities created by researchers such as Kamijō Tanenosuke and Inoue Tatsuo, it is evident that they concentrated in the San’in and Tōkai regions. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|de|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|zh|延喜式|qq=draft}}, there are 14 “Shitori Shrines” (倭文神社) recorded nationwide: 6 in the San’in region, 5 in Tōkaidō, 2 in Tōsandō, and 2 in the Kinai. Another 8 shrines related to Shitori but not recorded in the Engishiki exist, with 7 of them in Tōkaidō. In Inaba, both the “Engishiki” Jinmyōchō and the “{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|de|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名抄|zh|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}” note a “Shitori Shrine” (倭文神社) in Takakusa District. The “Heian Ibun” mentions a person named “Shitori Mahiro” (倭文真弘) from Takaniwa-shō. In both Inaba and Hōki, the sites of “Shitori Shrines” often occupy slightly elevated land near coasts, rivers, or lakes—this is true nationwide. Consequently, “Shitori communities” may have had close ties with “Ama (sea-people) communities,” and possibly with the {{ill|Hata clan|de|Hata-Klan|ja|秦氏|zh|秦氏|qq=draft}} in terms of weaving technology. Thus, Shitori communities in Takakusa District’s Takaniwa-shō and in Kawamura District most likely produced high-grade silk. While these high-end products were initially luxuries for the imperial court nobility, over time the technology spread to ordinary people.Kamijō Tanenosuke: “A Preliminary Study on Shitori Communities,” in the journal Nihon Rekishi (日本歴史)Inoue Tatsuo: “A Study of the Shōzei-chō (正税帳)” == Shaden and grounds == [[File:Sukunemizu-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|Shitori no Sukune Mizu]] The main hall is an ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} style with copper roof, rebuilt in Taishō 8 (1919). In the same year, a worship hall (two bays square) was also reconstructed, and the precincts were substantially expanded. The offering hall (幣殿), measuring two bays by one bay, was renovated in Shōwa 12 (1937), and a shrine kitchen (神饌殿) was built in that same year. In Shōwa 17 (1942), a purification font (手水舎) was constructed. There is also a lodging hall (参籠所) and a memorial shrine (祖霊舎) dedicated to the ancestors of parishioners. The precinct covers 747 tsubo (about 2,468 m²); in Shōwa 9 (1934), it measured 469 tsubo (about 1,550 m²). Within the grounds is “Shitori no Sukune Mizu,” said to be a miraculous water especially helpful for women’s health, though now its well is no longer in use. Additionally, near the large De River in front of the shrine there was once a sacred rice field (神饌田) used again as a ritual paddy after some time in private ownership. The first torii, donated by Okamura Ichizaemon in Meiji 2 (1869), is made of stone and stands about 3 meters tall and 2.4 meters wide; until recently it bore a hanging plaque (扁額) reading “倭文神社.” Records show that major reconstructions were carried out from Shōtoku 5 (1715) to Keiō 3 (1867) on 18 occasions, and the roof of the shrine buildings was replaced 13 times.Documents kept by the shrine priest’s family, “Records of Domain Shrine Holdings” (藩主社領帳) === Shrines and sub-shrines within the precincts === * Kaze-no-miya Sha (風宮社) – enshrines the guardian deities of protection from storm and flood. * Soreisha (祖霊舎) – enshrines the ancestors of the parishioners. == Treasures and writings == [[File:Houtou-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|The treasured sword offered by Lord {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}} of Kano Castle]] * One mirror (diameter ~12 cm): donated by Nishio Kihei, forged by Fujiwara Mitsunaga * One sword (total length about 48.5 cm): donated by Kamei Musashino-kami Norisuke ({{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}); believed to be from the Kamakura period * One hanging plaque (width about 90 cm) with raised lettering “倭文神社,” donated by Okamura Juzaemon * Writings: “Thoughts on Shitori no Kami” by Motoori Uchitō * Writings: “On the Deities of Shitori Shrine” by Udagawa Masayoshi == Local information == ; location * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori 548-1 ; Access * About 2 minutes by car from Tottori Minami IC on the {{ill|Tottori Expressway|de|Tottori-Autobahn|ja|鳥取自動車道|zh|鳥取自動車道|qq=draft}} ; neighborhood * {{ill|Kawahara Castle|de|Burg Kawahara|ja|河原城|zh|河原城|qq=draft}} == footnote == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten * Tottori Prefectural Local History * Tottori Prefectural History * Inaba-shi, p.433 * Genji 1 Gōson-chō * Tottori Domain History * Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) * Shitori Shrine Detailed Records “Inaba” * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1992|chapter=Shitori Shrine|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|de|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|zh|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft}} 32 Tottori-ken no Chimei|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|de|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|zh|平凡社|qq=draft}}|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Shitori Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) == See Also == * ''{{ill|Takahatsuchi-no-Mikoto|de|Takahatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|zh|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft}}'' {{Multiple issues |accuracy=2016-10 |notability=2016-10 }} {{Infobox Shinto shrine |name = '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) |image = [[File:Shitori-jinjya-totori-honden.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|26|25.6|N|134|12|1.9|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}{{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鸟取市|qq=draft}} Oaza Shitori Ie-no-Ue 548-1 |ISO = JP-31 |latitude = 35/26/25.6/N |longitude = 134/12/1.9/E |label = left |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}})
{{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}} |goshintai = |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}} |foundation = Unknown |main_hall = Ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} |other_names = |pilgrimage = |annual_festival = October 9 |events = |map = Japan Tottori }} '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto-Schrein|ja|神社|zh|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori area. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and under the modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines ({{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|de|Modernes System der Shinto-Schreineinstufung|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}) it was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}}. The shrine stands on the western slope of the mountain in the Shitori settlement, located in the southwestern part of Tottori City. == history == The exact date of the shrine’s founding (勧請) is unknown, but it is said to have originated when members of the {{ill|Shitori/Shizuri clan|de|Shitori/ Shizuri-Clan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} enshrined their ancestral deity. It is counted among the seven shrines of {{ill|Takakusa District|de|Takakusa Distrikt|ja|高草郡|zh|高草郡|qq=draft}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|zh|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}. In the medieval period and beyond, because the enshrined deity {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}}) was said to have seven names, it became known as the “Seven-Body Great Deity” (七躰大明神, Nanatai Daimyōjin) and was revered as the local tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|de|Ubusunagami|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|zh|産土神|qq=draft}}). During the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Takeda Takanobu|de|Takeda Takanobu|ja|武田高信|zh|武田高信|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Tamatsu Castle|de|Tamatsu Burg|ja|玉津城|zh|玉津城|qq=draft}}, built a “Three-foot” sub-shrine; in the Keichō era (1598–1615), {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Kano Castle (Tottori)|de|Burg Kano|ja|鹿野城|1=Shikano Castle|zh|鹿野城|qq=draft}}, offered a treasured sword; and in Kan’ei 9 (1632), domain lord {{ill|Ikeda Mitsunaka|de|Ikeda Mitsunaka|ja|池田光仲|zh|池田光仲|qq=draft}} donated 1 koku, 3 to, 1 shō, and 7 gō of shrine land. It thus gained reverence as the guardian deity of the southwestern direction (坤) of {{ill|Tottori Castle|de|Burg Tottori|ja|鳥取城|zh|鳥取城|qq=draft}}. During the Edo period, not only the feudal lords but also the common people held it in high regard. For instance, in the relocation of the shrine in Meiwa 1 (1764), the headman (大庄屋) contributed rice bales on behalf of the entire district, and in Bunka 7 (1810), the district commissioner (郡奉行) donated 100 momme of silver. Many local worshipers gathered at the Setsubun festival, and this popular devotion continued throughout the Inaba region until the early Shōwa period. In 1872 (Meiji 1), the shrine name was changed to “Shitori Jinja”; in 1874 (Meiji 4), it was Ranked as a village shrine; in 1915 (Taishō 4), it was designated a shrine for which the state offered shared expenses for rituals and offerings; in 1919 (Taishō 8), the main hall, hall of offerings, worship hall, and shrine office were renovated; and on October 1, 1943 (Shōwa 18), it was elevated to Gōsha status.Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) The word “Shitori” (also read “Shizuri”) refers to silk textiles. According to tradition, the {{ill|Shitori clan|de|Shitori-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}}, a community engaged in weaving, lived in this area and enshrined their ancestor deity, “Shitori no Kami / Takehazuchi no Mikoto.” The four deities enshrined are {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|經津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}}. In the myth “{{ill|The Hare of Inaba|de|Der Hase von Inaba|ja|因幡の白兎|zh|因幡の白兎|qq=draft}},” it is said that after helping the white hare, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto wrote a love letter here to the Princess Yagami. (It is believed that this actually refers to composing poetry at the time.) It is said that the love letter from Ōkuninushi no Mikoto was associated with the character for “文” (letter or text) and also with rolls of silk cloth.Shrine precinct information sign === Historical Origin === According to the “Continued Chronicles of Japan” ({{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}}), under the entry for Wadō 5 (712) in the seventh month of autumn, it is noted that brocade was woven in Inaba Province. From this, we can infer that even before this year, the descendants of the shrine’s deity {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, who were part of the {{ill|Shitori (Weaving) clan|de|Shitori (Weaving)-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} originally based in the Katsuragi area of Yamato Province and engaged in weaving, had traveled here. They established Shitori Village while continuing their ancestral weaving industry, and then founded this shrine to enshrine their ancestral deity, Takehazuchi no Mikoto.Shrine-owned “YuiShoGaki” (由緒書) === On the Shitori Organization in Inaba and Hōki Provinces === In ancient times, industrial production in {{ill|Inaba Province|de|Provinz Inaba|ja|因幡|zh|因幡|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōki Province|de|Provinz Hōki|ja|伯耆|zh|伯耆|qq=draft}} seems to have been relatively advanced, suggesting these areas were culturally developed. When examining sericulture and the production of silk among the farming population in Inaba and Hōki, it is important to note that these fabrics were produced as tribute ({{ill|Chō (tribute)|de|Chō (Abgabe)|ja|調|zh|調|qq=draft}}), not for daily use by ordinary farmers. Concerning the production of high-grade silk textiles, the entry of Wadō 5 (712) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}} for the seventh month, day of jinwu (壬午), lists 21 provinces, including Ise Province. Both Inaba and Hōki are among them. This confirms that the production of brocade and figured silk (“綾”) was flourishing in Inaba and Hōki from ancient times. “錦部” (Nishikibe) communities, deeply involved in producing brocade and figured silk, can be inferred from the existence of a person named “Nishikibe Shinafu-me” (錦部志奈布女) in Takaniwa-shō in Inaba Province (cited in “Heian Ibun”). Furthermore, the cloth known as “倭文布” (Shizuri or Shitori cloth), said to be a striped textile, also proves the existence of Shitori (倭文) organizations in ancient times. From nationwide distribution maps of Shitori communities created by researchers such as Kamijō Tanenosuke and Inoue Tatsuo, it is evident that they concentrated in the San’in and Tōkai regions. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|de|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|zh|延喜式|qq=draft}}, there are 14 “Shitori Shrines” (倭文神社) recorded nationwide: 6 in the San’in region, 5 in Tōkaidō, 2 in Tōsandō, and 2 in the Kinai. Another 8 shrines related to Shitori but not recorded in the Engishiki exist, with 7 of them in Tōkaidō. In Inaba, both the “Engishiki” Jinmyōchō and the “{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|de|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名抄|zh|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}” note a “Shitori Shrine” (倭文神社) in Takakusa District. The “Heian Ibun” mentions a person named “Shitori Mahiro” (倭文真弘) from Takaniwa-shō. In both Inaba and Hōki, the sites of “Shitori Shrines” often occupy slightly elevated land near coasts, rivers, or lakes—this is true nationwide. Consequently, “Shitori communities” may have had close ties with “Ama (sea-people) communities,” and possibly with the {{ill|Hata clan|de|Hata-Klan|ja|秦氏|zh|秦氏|qq=draft}} in terms of weaving technology. Thus, Shitori communities in Takakusa District’s Takaniwa-shō and in Kawamura District most likely produced high-grade silk. While these high-end products were initially luxuries for the imperial court nobility, over time the technology spread to ordinary people.Kamijō Tanenosuke: “A Preliminary Study on Shitori Communities,” in the journal Nihon Rekishi (日本歴史)Inoue Tatsuo: “A Study of the Shōzei-chō (正税帳)” == Shaden and grounds == [[File:Sukunemizu-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|Shitori no Sukune Mizu]] The main hall is an ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} style with copper roof, rebuilt in Taishō 8 (1919). In the same year, a worship hall (two bays square) was also reconstructed, and the precincts were substantially expanded. The offering hall (幣殿), measuring two bays by one bay, was renovated in Shōwa 12 (1937), and a shrine kitchen (神饌殿) was built in that same year. In Shōwa 17 (1942), a purification font (手水舎) was constructed. There is also a lodging hall (参籠所) and a memorial shrine (祖霊舎) dedicated to the ancestors of parishioners. The precinct covers 747 tsubo (about 2,468 m²); in Shōwa 9 (1934), it measured 469 tsubo (about 1,550 m²). Within the grounds is “Shitori no Sukune Mizu,” said to be a miraculous water especially helpful for women’s health, though now its well is no longer in use. Additionally, near the large De River in front of the shrine there was once a sacred rice field (神饌田) used again as a ritual paddy after some time in private ownership. The first torii, donated by Okamura Ichizaemon in Meiji 2 (1869), is made of stone and stands about 3 meters tall and 2.4 meters wide; until recently it bore a hanging plaque (扁額) reading “倭文神社.” Records show that major reconstructions were carried out from Shōtoku 5 (1715) to Keiō 3 (1867) on 18 occasions, and the roof of the shrine buildings was replaced 13 times.Documents kept by the shrine priest’s family, “Records of Domain Shrine Holdings” (藩主社領帳) === Shrines and sub-shrines within the precincts === * Kaze-no-miya Sha (風宮社) – enshrines the guardian deities of protection from storm and flood. * Soreisha (祖霊舎) – enshrines the ancestors of the parishioners. == Treasures and writings == [[File:Houtou-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|The treasured sword offered by Lord {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}} of Kano Castle]] * One mirror (diameter ~12 cm): donated by Nishio Kihei, forged by Fujiwara Mitsunaga * One sword (total length about 48.5 cm): donated by Kamei Musashino-kami Norisuke ({{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}); believed to be from the Kamakura period * One hanging plaque (width about 90 cm) with raised lettering “倭文神社,” donated by Okamura Juzaemon * Writings: “Thoughts on Shitori no Kami” by Motoori Uchitō * Writings: “On the Deities of Shitori Shrine” by Udagawa Masayoshi == Local information == ; location * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori 548-1 ; Access * About 2 minutes by car from Tottori Minami IC on the {{ill|Tottori Expressway|de|Tottori-Autobahn|ja|鳥取自動車道|zh|鳥取自動車道|qq=draft}} ; neighborhood * {{ill|Kawahara Castle|de|Burg Kawahara|ja|河原城|zh|河原城|qq=draft}} == footnote == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten * Tottori Prefectural Local History * Tottori Prefectural History * Inaba-shi, p.433 * Genji 1 Gōson-chō * Tottori Domain History * Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) * Shitori Shrine Detailed Records “Inaba” * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1992|chapter=Shitori Shrine|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|de|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|zh|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft}} 32 Tottori-ken no Chimei|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|de|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|zh|平凡社|qq=draft}}|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Shitori Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) == See Also == * {{Multiple issues |accuracy=2016-10 |notability=2016-10 }} {{Infobox Shinto shrine |name = '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) |image = [[File:Shitori-jinjya-totori-honden.jpg|270px]]
Main Hall
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|26|25.6|N|134|12|1.9|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}{{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鸟取市|qq=draft}} Oaza Shitori Ie-no-Ue 548-1 |ISO = JP-31 |latitude = 35/26/25.6/N |longitude = 134/12/1.9/E |label = left |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}})
{{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}} |goshintai = |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}} |foundation = Unknown |main_hall = Ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} |other_names = |pilgrimage = |annual_festival = October 9 |events = |map = Japan Tottori }} '''Shitori Shrine''' (倭文神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto-Schrein|ja|神社|zh|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori area. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|de|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and under the modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines ({{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|de|Modernes System der Shinto-Schreineinstufung|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}) it was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|de|Gōsha|ja|郷社|zh|郷社|qq=draft}}. The shrine stands on the western slope of the mountain in the Shitori settlement, located in the southwestern part of Tottori City. == history == The exact date of the shrine’s founding (勧請) is unknown, but it is said to have originated when members of the {{ill|Shitori/Shizuri clan|de|Shitori/ Shizuri-Clan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} enshrined their ancestral deity. It is counted among the seven shrines of {{ill|Takakusa District|de|Takakusa Distrikt|ja|高草郡|zh|高草郡|qq=draft}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|zh|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}}. In the medieval period and beyond, because the enshrined deity {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|de|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|zh|大国主命|qq=draft}}) was said to have seven names, it became known as the “Seven-Body Great Deity” (七躰大明神, Nanatai Daimyōjin) and was revered as the local tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|de|Ubusunagami|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|zh|産土神|qq=draft}}). During the Tenshō era (1573–1592), {{ill|Takeda Takanobu|de|Takeda Takanobu|ja|武田高信|zh|武田高信|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Tamatsu Castle|de|Tamatsu Burg|ja|玉津城|zh|玉津城|qq=draft}}, built a “Three-foot” sub-shrine; in the Keichō era (1598–1615), {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}, lord of {{ill|Kano Castle (Tottori)|de|Burg Kano|ja|鹿野城|1=Shikano Castle|zh|鹿野城|qq=draft}}, offered a treasured sword; and in Kan’ei 9 (1632), domain lord {{ill|Ikeda Mitsunaka|de|Ikeda Mitsunaka|ja|池田光仲|zh|池田光仲|qq=draft}} donated 1 koku, 3 to, 1 shō, and 7 gō of shrine land. It thus gained reverence as the guardian deity of the southwestern direction (坤) of {{ill|Tottori Castle|de|Burg Tottori|ja|鳥取城|zh|鳥取城|qq=draft}}. During the Edo period, not only the feudal lords but also the common people held it in high regard. For instance, in the relocation of the shrine in Meiwa 1 (1764), the headman (大庄屋) contributed rice bales on behalf of the entire district, and in Bunka 7 (1810), the district commissioner (郡奉行) donated 100 momme of silver. Many local worshipers gathered at the Setsubun festival, and this popular devotion continued throughout the Inaba region until the early Shōwa period. In 1872 (Meiji 1), the shrine name was changed to “Shitori Jinja”; in 1874 (Meiji 4), it was Ranked as a village shrine; in 1915 (Taishō 4), it was designated a shrine for which the state offered shared expenses for rituals and offerings; in 1919 (Taishō 8), the main hall, hall of offerings, worship hall, and shrine office were renovated; and on October 1, 1943 (Shōwa 18), it was elevated to Gōsha status.Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) The word “Shitori” (also read “Shizuri”) refers to silk textiles. According to tradition, the {{ill|Shitori clan|de|Shitori-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}}, a community engaged in weaving, lived in this area and enshrined their ancestor deity, “Shitori no Kami / Takehazuchi no Mikoto.” The four deities enshrined are {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|de|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大己貴命|zh|大己貴命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Kami|de|Futsunushi no Kami|ja|經津主神|zh|経津主神|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Takemikazuchi no Kami|de|Takemikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌命|zh|武甕槌命|qq=draft}}. In the myth “{{ill|The Hare of Inaba|de|Der Hase von Inaba|ja|因幡の白兎|zh|因幡の白兎|qq=draft}},” it is said that after helping the white hare, Ōkuninushi no Mikoto wrote a love letter here to the Princess Yagami. (It is believed that this actually refers to composing poetry at the time.) It is said that the love letter from Ōkuninushi no Mikoto was associated with the character for “文” (letter or text) and also with rolls of silk cloth.Shrine precinct information sign === Historical Origin === According to the “Continued Chronicles of Japan” ({{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}}), under the entry for Wadō 5 (712) in the seventh month of autumn, it is noted that brocade was woven in Inaba Province. From this, we can infer that even before this year, the descendants of the shrine’s deity {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|zh|建葉槌命|qq=draft}}, who were part of the {{ill|Shitori (Weaving) clan|de|Shitori (Weaving)-Klan|ja|倭文部|1=Shizuri clan|2=ja|3=倭文氏|4=zh|5=倭文氏|6=de|7=Shizuri Clan|8=fr|9=Clan Shizuri|lt=Shizuri Bemin (vassals of the Shizuri clan)|zh|倭文部|qq=draft}} originally based in the Katsuragi area of Yamato Province and engaged in weaving, had traveled here. They established Shitori Village while continuing their ancestral weaving industry, and then founded this shrine to enshrine their ancestral deity, Takehazuchi no Mikoto.Shrine-owned “YuiShoGaki” (由緒書) === On the Shitori Organization in Inaba and Hōki Provinces === In ancient times, industrial production in {{ill|Inaba Province|de|Provinz Inaba|ja|因幡|zh|因幡|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hōki Province|de|Provinz Hōki|ja|伯耆|zh|伯耆|qq=draft}} seems to have been relatively advanced, suggesting these areas were culturally developed. When examining sericulture and the production of silk among the farming population in Inaba and Hōki, it is important to note that these fabrics were produced as tribute ({{ill|Chō (tribute)|de|Chō (Abgabe)|ja|調|zh|調|qq=draft}}), not for daily use by ordinary farmers. Concerning the production of high-grade silk textiles, the entry of Wadō 5 (712) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|de|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|zh|続日本紀|qq=draft}} for the seventh month, day of jinwu (壬午), lists 21 provinces, including Ise Province. Both Inaba and Hōki are among them. This confirms that the production of brocade and figured silk (“綾”) was flourishing in Inaba and Hōki from ancient times. “錦部” (Nishikibe) communities, deeply involved in producing brocade and figured silk, can be inferred from the existence of a person named “Nishikibe Shinafu-me” (錦部志奈布女) in Takaniwa-shō in Inaba Province (cited in “Heian Ibun”). Furthermore, the cloth known as “倭文布” (Shizuri or Shitori cloth), said to be a striped textile, also proves the existence of Shitori (倭文) organizations in ancient times. From nationwide distribution maps of Shitori communities created by researchers such as Kamijō Tanenosuke and Inoue Tatsuo, it is evident that they concentrated in the San’in and Tōkai regions. According to the {{ill|Engishiki|de|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|zh|延喜式|qq=draft}}, there are 14 “Shitori Shrines” (倭文神社) recorded nationwide: 6 in the San’in region, 5 in Tōkaidō, 2 in Tōsandō, and 2 in the Kinai. Another 8 shrines related to Shitori but not recorded in the Engishiki exist, with 7 of them in Tōkaidō. In Inaba, both the “Engishiki” Jinmyōchō and the “{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|de|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名抄|zh|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}” note a “Shitori Shrine” (倭文神社) in Takakusa District. The “Heian Ibun” mentions a person named “Shitori Mahiro” (倭文真弘) from Takaniwa-shō. In both Inaba and Hōki, the sites of “Shitori Shrines” often occupy slightly elevated land near coasts, rivers, or lakes—this is true nationwide. Consequently, “Shitori communities” may have had close ties with “Ama (sea-people) communities,” and possibly with the {{ill|Hata clan|de|Hata-Klan|ja|秦氏|zh|秦氏|qq=draft}} in terms of weaving technology. Thus, Shitori communities in Takakusa District’s Takaniwa-shō and in Kawamura District most likely produced high-grade silk. While these high-end products were initially luxuries for the imperial court nobility, over time the technology spread to ordinary people.Kamijō Tanenosuke: “A Preliminary Study on Shitori Communities,” in the journal Nihon Rekishi (日本歴史)Inoue Tatsuo: “A Study of the Shōzei-chō (正税帳)” == Shaden and grounds == [[File:Sukunemizu-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|Shitori no Sukune Mizu]] The main hall is an ichigen-sha {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|de|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|zh|流造|qq=draft}} style with copper roof, rebuilt in Taishō 8 (1919). In the same year, a worship hall (two bays square) was also reconstructed, and the precincts were substantially expanded. The offering hall (幣殿), measuring two bays by one bay, was renovated in Shōwa 12 (1937), and a shrine kitchen (神饌殿) was built in that same year. In Shōwa 17 (1942), a purification font (手水舎) was constructed. There is also a lodging hall (参籠所) and a memorial shrine (祖霊舎) dedicated to the ancestors of parishioners. The precinct covers 747 tsubo (about 2,468 m²); in Shōwa 9 (1934), it measured 469 tsubo (about 1,550 m²). Within the grounds is “Shitori no Sukune Mizu,” said to be a miraculous water especially helpful for women’s health, though now its well is no longer in use. Additionally, near the large De River in front of the shrine there was once a sacred rice field (神饌田) used again as a ritual paddy after some time in private ownership. The first torii, donated by Okamura Ichizaemon in Meiji 2 (1869), is made of stone and stands about 3 meters tall and 2.4 meters wide; until recently it bore a hanging plaque (扁額) reading “倭文神社.” Records show that major reconstructions were carried out from Shōtoku 5 (1715) to Keiō 3 (1867) on 18 occasions, and the roof of the shrine buildings was replaced 13 times.Documents kept by the shrine priest’s family, “Records of Domain Shrine Holdings” (藩主社領帳) === Shrines and sub-shrines within the precincts === * Kaze-no-miya Sha (風宮社) – enshrines the guardian deities of protection from storm and flood. * Soreisha (祖霊舎) – enshrines the ancestors of the parishioners. == Treasures and writings == [[File:Houtou-shitori-tottori.png|thumb|The treasured sword offered by Lord {{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}} of Kano Castle]] * One mirror (diameter ~12 cm): donated by Nishio Kihei, forged by Fujiwara Mitsunaga * One sword (total length about 48.5 cm): donated by Kamei Musashino-kami Norisuke ({{ill|Kamei Norisuke|de|Kamei Norisuke|ja|亀井茲矩|zh|亀井茲矩|qq=draft}}); believed to be from the Kamakura period * One hanging plaque (width about 90 cm) with raised lettering “倭文神社,” donated by Okamura Juzaemon * Writings: “Thoughts on Shitori no Kami” by Motoori Uchitō * Writings: “On the Deities of Shitori Shrine” by Udagawa Masayoshi == Local information == ; location * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|de|Präfektur Tottori|ja|鳥取県|zh|鸟取县|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tottori|de|Tottori|ja|鳥取市|zh|鳥取市|qq=draft}} Shitori 548-1 ; Access * About 2 minutes by car from Tottori Minami IC on the {{ill|Tottori Expressway|de|Tottori-Autobahn|ja|鳥取自動車道|zh|鳥取自動車道|qq=draft}} ; neighborhood * {{ill|Kawahara Castle|de|Burg Kawahara|ja|河原城|zh|河原城|qq=draft}} == footnote Tottori Prefectural Shrine Magazine (鳥取県神社誌) * Shitori Shrine Detailed Records “Inaba” * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1992|chapter=Shitori Shrine|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|de|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|zh|日本歴史地名大系|qq=draft}} 32 Tottori-ken no Chimei|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|de|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|zh|平凡社|qq=draft}}|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Shitori Shrine (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor={{ill|Shikinaisha Research Council|de|Shikinaisha Forschungsgruppe|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft研究会|qq=draft}}|author=Yamamoto Hiroshi|chapter=Shitori Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol.19|publisher={{ill|Kōgakkan University|de|Kōgakkan-Universität|ja|皇學館大学|zh|皇學館大学|qq=draft}} Publishing Department|pages=1095-1099|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Suiwakahime no Mikoto Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1984}}}} == External links == * [http://shitori-jinjya.main.jp/ Shitori Shrine | Tottori City] (official page) == See Also == *
